headlines.title: MAYOR QUESTIONS CHARTER'S LEGALITY; TIES UP WARRANTS!
headlines.subtitle: Orders City Secretary Not to Deliver Checks to Former Mayor and Attorney of North Fort Worth for Part of Month's Service.
headlines.fullText:
Mayor W. D. Harris Saturday morning instructed City Secretary Estes not to deliver to former Mayor Grant and former City Attor- hey John W. Baskin of North Fort Worth warrants for their salaries as city commissioner and assistant city attorney respectively.
The ground for the mayor's action is that he does not think the att of the legislature in naming them as officials in the new city charter is legal.
Mayor Harris also questions the validity of the charter provi- som requiring the city to issue $200,000 in bonds for improvements in the territory formerly included in North Fort Worth. In a com- munication to City Attorney Samuels he has raised both of these questions and asks for a legal opinion regarding the same.

headlines.title: New Pastor of First Christian Preaches First Sermon Sunday
headlines.fullText:
R EV. J. E. DINGER, the new pastor of the First Christian church of this city, has gained wide prominence as a lecturer and author in addition to his sue- cess as- a pastor. For two years Rev. Mr. Dinger was un- der contract of a Kansas City lyceum bureau and lectured throughout the South and Southwest. He has also been a frequent contributor of spe- cial articles to newspapers and magazines.
He comes to Fort Worth from Chickasha, Okla., where in three years' time the mem- bership of his congregation was doubled through his untir- ing efforts. Rev. Mr. Dinger will preach his first sermon at the 11 o'clock service Sunday.

headlines.title: FIERCE FIRE SWEEPS SOUTH SIDE; SEVEN ENTIRE BLOCKS BURN; LOSS OVER $2,000,000
headlines.subtitle: STARTING AT THE CORNER OF ST. LOUIS AND PETERSMITH STREETS AT 1:30 O'CLOCK IN A HOUSE THAT WAS CLOSED UP, WHAT WILL BE THE MOST DISASTROUS FIRE IN THE HISTORY OF FORT WORTH HAD WIPED OUT SEVEN SQUARE BLOCKS OF FINE RESIDENCES UP TO 3'O'CLOCK SATURDAY AFTER- NOON AND WAS STILL RAGING WITH UNCONTR ILLABLE FURY.
headlines.fullText:
DESTROYED:
Seven blocks between Railroad Avenue and Tucker Street, Jennings avenue to South Main streets.
Three handsome churches, the Broadway Baptist, the Broadway Presbyterian and the Swedish church. T. & P. Roundhouse. Fully 100 residences. The loss will aggregate $2,000,000.
The fire department is helpless; aid has been summoned from Dallas.
THE FLAMES ORIGINATED IN THE HOME OF MRS. HATCHER WHILE THE ENTIRE FAMILY WAS AT THE CITY PARK PIONI KING. IT LEAPED ACROSS PETER SMITH TO THE HOME OF MRS. L. C. HUTOR INS, THENCE TO BROADWAY, TAKING IN ITS PATH TWO CHURCHES. FROM BROADWAY, THE FIRE SPREAD TO RAILROAD AVENUE, THENCE DOWN TO SOUTH MAIN
ALL OF THE HOUSES ON THE SOUTH SIDE OF P STER SMITH BETWEEN JENNINGS AVENUE AND ST. LOUIS AVENUE, WERE BURNED TO THE GROUND, SCARCELY A POST STANDING.
These houses, four in number, on the south side of Peter Saith, were owned by N. Shanblum. No contents were saved from any of these. On the east side of peter Smith, the first house destroyed belonged to Mrs. L. C. Hutchins, a widow. Immediately west of her home, the residence of Mrs. Stallings was partly burned.
Going across to Broadway, the flames took in their path the nenses of Mrs. A. F. Conless, George Neumagen, the Broadway Baptist church, George Laneri's residence, the Swedish church and all their outhouses.
The Broadway Presbyterian church was afire at 3 o'clock and seemed doomed.
Four rent houses, owned by William Bell, a railroad man, were taken in succession on Broadway between St. Louis and Galveston. The homes of George Monnig, W. S. Polhemus, W. S. Bogert, Ed- ward Burns, Peter Reagan. W. M. Hanney and David Tobin on Broadway are apparently doomed. From the Bells houses the flames spread north to Railroad avenue, threatening the Modern apart- ments and other big buildings.
On Railroad avenue, beginning at the corner of St. Louis, the following honses were either burned or were burning at 3 o'clock: John Temes, R. J. Harkelroad, W. P. Hall, Dr. J. 24, Mullins, M. E. Roberts.
Indications at 2 o'clock were that the fire could not be stopped by human means south of Rail- road avenne nor east of Main. At that time the flames were springing from scores of houses all over the section from Railroad avenue south to Peter Smith and east of Jennings avenue with a few excep- tions along the southwest part of that ikea.
The railroad reservation is counted upon to prevent a spread to the business section of the city and Main street may afford a fighting ground. from which the firemen may make a final stand.
If it passes Main street to a considerable, extent there seems no chance to stop it until the railroads intersecting the city east of Main street are reached. The strong wind blowing from the southwest is carrying the flames in sheets across streets and vacant lots, sweeping all before.
On St. Louis avenue, goods were be- Ing removed from all houses between Railroad avenue and Tucker streets.

headlines.title: KILLED POLICEMAN; INDICTMENT REFUSED
headlines.subtitle: Grand Jury Refuses to Return True Bill-Officer Was Forcing Entrance.
headlines.fullText:
By Associated Press. NEW YORK. April 3. The grand jury yesterday refused to Indict Edward J. Byrnes, who on Feb. 15 shot and killed Police Sergeant John B. Goldhammer, as the latter was fore- ing an entrance, into Brynes' apart- ments in search of evidence for use in certain police prosecutions then under way.
The case attracted wide attention be- cause of Brynes' plea that Goldhammer had no warrant and he belleved the of- fleer was a burglar.

headlines.title: GETS 99 YEARS FOR KILLING PHONE MAN
headlines.subtitle: Because He Was Presented With an Unpaid Hill, Drunken Man Shet Manager of Exchange
